findings.title: Governance rotation leaves former avatar with permanent reserve minting and helper admin powers

best_agent_match.title: Missing Zero Address Validation in setDistributionHelper

findings.title: Public address refresh makes the hardcoded guardian effectively irrevocable

best_agent_match.title: A hardcoded guardian can always be re-granted by any caller

findings.title: Anyone can trigger zero-slippage fee-restocking sales of protocol-owned G$

best_agent_match.title: Public fee-restocking path sells protocol G$ with zero slippage protection
Reentrant contract recipients can recursively re-distribute funds via `transferAndCall`

findings.title: Unchecked oracle answers can halt `collectInterest` and misprice keeper rewards

best_agent_match.title: Unchecked cDai Redeem and Mint Return Values

rejected_candidates.title: Unchecked Call Return Value in ERC20 transferFrom
rejected_candidates.reason: Not a practical issue here: the reserve buy path uses fixed cDAI/DAI integrations rather than arbitrary ERC20s, so generic non-standard token compatibility does not create an exploitable protocol vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Access Control on onDistribution Function
rejected_candidates.reason: Public triggering is explicitly intended by the contract comments. Standalone timing control is not reportable by itself; the real issue is the merged zero-slippage fee sale that public callers can trigger.

rejected_candidates.title: Unlimited Token Approvals Set by Anyone
rejected_candidates.reason: `setAddresses()` only refreshes approvals to addresses resolved from `NameService`, which is avatar-controlled. The public refresh function alone does not let an attacker redirect approvals to themselves.

rejected_candidates.title: Division by Zero in GoodMarketMaker.calculateMintInterest
rejected_candidates.reason: The reported path depends on privileged misconfiguration or an undeveloped reserve-depletion scenario. The agent did not demonstrate a concrete, realistic permissionless exploit from the current code.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Input Validation in GoodMarketMaker.initializeToken
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a governance-parameter sanity concern, not a demonstrated exploit path or user-reachable vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Potential Integer Overflow in BancorFormula
rejected_candidates.reason: Too speculative. Solidity 0.8 has checked arithmetic, and no concrete overflow-triggering input path or profit mechanism was shown.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Pausable in DistributionHelper
rejected_candidates.reason: Absence of an emergency pause is a design preference, not a concrete security bug in itself.

rejected_candidates.title: Unchecked cDai Redeem and Mint Return Values
rejected_candidates.reason: The contract does check the Compound return codes and reverts on failure. Generic error strings are not a security issue.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Zero Address Validation in setDistributionHelper
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a recoverable avatar misconfiguration, not an exploitable vulnerability path.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Deadline Check in Uniswap Swaps
rejected_candidates.reason: Using `block.timestamp` as a same-transaction deadline is standard router usage and does not trap user funds in this flow.

rejected_candidates.title: Hardcoded Oracle Address in DistributionHelper
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a maintainability/centralization concern rather than a concrete exploit that causes realistic protocol harm by itself.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Validation of Array Length in collectInterest
rejected_candidates.reason: Duplicate staking addresses were not shown to create double-crediting, theft, or a realistic protocol-level failure from the current logic.

rejected_candidates.title: Inconsistent NonReentrant Implementation
rejected_candidates.reason: Implementation style only. The presence of custom reentrancy guards instead of OpenZeppelin's library is not a reportable vulnerability.
